The case of enhanced star formation in VV 499 probably has a relation to statistics of distant dwarf satellites of massive spirals, studied by Zaritsky et al. ( ). Most of those satellites are emission-line galaxies. We suggest that the likely trigger of current starburst in VV 499 is its tidal disturbance by M 81 subgroup(s) or/and interaction with intragroup gas.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC4459 as an extreme high outlier, with simulations failing to reach its observed cluster-formation level in the young age range.

quotes.quote: The high outliers in the _ SFR relationship range from galaxies with no overlap in the 1000 simulations for three galaxies (UGC5336, UGC4459) to high values in the < 10 Myr age range, and one (UGC5692) in the < 100 Myr age range. These galaxies fall noticeably above the simulations. None of the simulations for UGC5336 nor UGC4459 and less than 1 and 7 of the simulations for UGC8760 and UGC5692, respectively, produced values as high as the observed data points. Since 1000 simulations were run, the lack of overlap with UGC5336 and UGC4459 make these galaxies >3 outliers. These galaxies provide strong evidence that more clusters are formed than expected from _ SFR relationship.
Furthermore, SLUG produced zero simulated cluster populations with values as high as the observed data points for UGC5336 and UGC4459 in the < 10 Myr age range.

sources.summary: The paper maps DDO 53 ionized gas, finding high velocity dispersion in faint diffuse regions and identifying shell-like structures, including a roughly 240 pc shell.

quotes.quote: DDO 53 The kinematics of ionized gas in this galaxy was previously investigated with the scanning FPI by Dicaire2008 , but the authors were only able to measure the distribution of radial velocities in the bright HII regions, whereas our data include the regions of faint diffuse emission. The map clearly reveals a pattern, typical of all galaxies we observed: the minimum value of velocity dispersion is seen in the centers of bright HII regions, while in the space between the star formation regions and in the outer parts of the galaxy reaches its maximal values up to 40-50 , which clearly points to the supersonic nature of motions. The shape of the I- diagram resembles the scheme, given in the paper by MunozTunon1996 - a horizontal lane (marked in dark blue in the Fig.  fig1_1 ) with _m and several inclined lanes with much larger values.

The best example of such a shell is given by a structure, adjacent to the north of the brightest HII region, in the diagram it is coloured yellow (centre) and dark green (periphery). This shell-like structure is also evident in the surface brightness distribution. In the figure we marked it as 1, its diameter is approximately d 14 arcsec (240 pc).

sources.summary: The paper highlights DD053 among the lowest-luminosity nearby XMPs, describing this low-surface-brightness subset as unusually gas-rich and inefficient at converting H i gas into stars.

quotes.quote: We call attention to the six sources with the lowest g band luminosity -- UGC2684, DD053, LeoA, SextansB, UGCA292 and GR8. These are all nearby (D < 6 Mpc), low-surface brightness galaxies, that also turn out to be XMPs.

We find an anti-correlation between the M _ H i /L _g ratio and the luminosity, whereby fainter XMPs are more gas-rich than brighter XMPs. The result suggests that the less luminous sources have converted a smaller fraction of their H i gas into stars. However, our sources deviate from the observed correlation found for dwarf galaxies (cyan line; bottom; Fig. 5; Staveley-Smith, Davies Kinman 1992) particularly at low luminosities, which correspond to the low-surface brightness, nearby galaxies UGC2684, DD053, LeoA, SextansB, UGCA292 and GR8. In this low luminosity regime, the XMP galaxies appear to be 10 to 100 times more gas-rich than typical dwarf galaxies.

sources.summary: UGC 4459 has double-peaked quasi-symmetric H I with a central depression, secondary-disk enhancement, gas nearly perpendicular to optical emission, and an aligned velocity gradient.

quotes.quote: UGC 4459 (also known as DDO 053 or VII Zw 238) From Walter et al. (2008; their Fig. 17), from VLA THINGS, and Begum et al. (2006; their Fig. 4), with the GMRT. The H 1 map at intermediate resolution (29 x 27 ) shows a double-peaked quasi-symmetric spherical structure, with a small extension to the Northeast. The two H 1 peaks are roughly coincident with the opposite ends of the optical main disk, with an H 1 depression in between. There is slight H 1 enhancement associated with the secondary optical disk. The overall position angle of the H 1 emission (PA _ HI 150 ^ ) is approximately perpendicular to the optical emission (PA _ opt 55 ^ ). The velocity gradient direction (PA _ VG 45 ^ ) is relatively well-aligned with the optical morphology.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 53’s smooth rotation curve, possible outer perturbations, BEC agreement, and a BEC mass estimate close to the observed galaxy mass.

quotes.quote: DDO 53: The rotation curve of the galaxy DDO 53 shows a clear and smooth rotational pattern, even that the possibility of external perturbations for >2 kpc cannot be excluded. These perturbations may be responsible for the sudden decay of the curve, without the presence of a significant plateau. However, the BEC prediction describes well the rotation curve. The mass of the galaxy is obtained as M_ HOII ^ BEC =0.55 10^ 9 M_ , while the observed mass is M=0.45 10^9M_ .

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 53 as an M81 Group member where seven H I holes were found, more than twice the number in prior work.

quotes.quote: DDO 53 DDO 53 (PGC 24050, UGC 4459, VIIZw 238) is another M81 Group member located 3.6 Mpc away with V-band magnitude 13.8 . We found a Type 1 hole and six Type 3 holes in the galaxy which is more than double the number detected by . The estimate of the scale height of the galaxy is 470 pc.

gmw_00000837.name: DDO 39
gmw_00000837.overview: DDO 39 is an irregular dwarf [arXiv:1911.00369] low surface brightness galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0303052, arXiv:1911.00369] that does not appear to be interacting [arXiv:astro-ph/0303052]. It exhibits noncircular motions in its central regions [arXiv:astro-ph/0303052, arXiv:1105.2996], which lack a strong bar or spiral arms [arXiv:astro-ph/0303052].

sources.summary: The paper finds DDO 39 has central noncircular motions and fiber-to-fiber velocity dispersion that make the circular velocity curve uncertain while remaining compatible with CDM inner slopes.

quotes.quote: The high-resolution velocity field of the LSB galaxy DDO 39 presented here reveals the presence of noncircular motions in the central regions and an intrinsic fiber-to-fiber dispersion of about 8 . The origin of either component is unclear; DDO 39 does not have a strong bar or spiral arms, nor does it appear to be interacting. Likely contributors are: star formation activity, turbulence, and small scale structure in the disk or dark halo. Irrespective of their origin, the noncircular motions and the large / _ in the galaxy center make an accurate measurement of the circular velocity curve difficult. As a result, inner slopes in the range 0< 1 are consistent with the data, and DDO 39 is compatible with a wide range of dark matter properties.
Specifically, it appears that the data presented here are consistent with the inner slopes expected in the currently popular CDM paradigm.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 39’s inner and outer non-circular motions, says the BEC model fits the mostly rotational middle and outer regions, and gives a BEC mass estimate.

quotes.quote: DDO 39: The rotation curve of the galaxy DDO 39 shows the presence of significant non-circular motions in the inner galactic region with <1.8 kpc. A strong gravitational perturbation may be responsible for the distortion of the inner part of the rotation curve. Other non-circular motions may be present in the outer region of the curve, for >9 kpc. The BEC model gives a relatively good description of the observational data in the middle and outer regions, where the motion is mostly rotational. The NFW fit gives a good description of the rotation curve.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 3371 as an irregular dwarf LSB galaxy whose HI and H rotation data disagree, while best-fit models favor a cuspy CDM halo.

quotes.quote: UGC 3371 (DDO 039) : UGC 3371 is an irregular dwarf galaxy (Irr). Observational studies indicate that there is an excellent agreement between the HI and H flat rotation curve velocities but the initial part of the HI rotation curve rises more steeply than the H one. This inconsistency in observational data could come from the overcorrection in beam smearing of HI data swaters2003 and because of this disagreement it is not possible to strongly predict the nature of its rotation curve, it could have either a linear rise or a steep rising. There are some studies such as swaters1999 which indicate that the best-fit models of UGC 3371 prefer a DM halo with a steep central cusp. The halo profile of UGC 3371 is hence consistent with CDM models bosch .

sources.summary: The paper notes that 3371's H-alpha curve rises more steeply than the H I curve, while later integral-field spectroscopy agrees with the H I rotation curve.

quotes.quote: 3371 . The \ rotation curve rises more steeply than the \ rotation curve. The reason for this difference is unclear. Swaters et al. \ (2003b) obtained an H rotation curve derived from integral field spectroscopy and find a good match between their \ rotation curve and our \ rotation curve.

gmw_00000838.name: J1319+0950
gmw_00000838.overview: J1319+0950 is an optically bright quasar host galaxy at a redshift of approximately 6.13 [arXiv:1912.05548, arXiv:2401.04211] that exhibits a clear velocity gradient in its gas disk [arXiv:1302.4154]. The system contains a massive reservoir of dust [arXiv:1709.04954, arXiv:2401.04211] and shows evidence of a modest molecular outflow [arXiv:1912.05548].

sources.summary: J1319+0950 is a z=6.127 QSO host with FIR and dust estimates; its tilted-ring fit agrees with prior dynamical-mass measurements despite non-elliptical outer rings.

quotes.quote: J1319+0950 ULAS J131911.29+095051.4 (J1319+0950; z=6.127 )is a QSO detected in the UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey (UKIDSS) mort09 . By fitting an optically thin greybody model with T _ dust =47 K and =1.6 to their FIR SED, wang11 estimate L_ FIR = 1.0 10^ 13 L _ . Using this FIR luminosity and an altered form of equation 1 of bian13 , they find a dust mass of 5.7 10^8 M _ .

J1319+0950 Similarly to J1000+0234, J1319+0950 shows a local velocity decrease at the penultimate point in its rotation curve. Due to the non-elliptical shape of each galaxy, the outer rings are fit only to a few data points, instead of the full annuli available to the inner rings. However, as seen in Figure MP , these local velocity minima do not translate to unphysical local dynamical mass minima. shao17 fit a tilted ring model to this galaxy in a similar fashion, resulting in nearly identical parameters. In addition, they found a dynamical mass of log _ 10 M_ dyn =11.1 0.1 , which agrees with the mass found through our analysis.

sources.summary: QSO J131911+095051 is the z=6.13 ALMA target with a tentative blueshifted OH absorption feature, a modest 200 solar-mass-per-year molecular outflow, and possible subsiding mechanical feedback.

quotes.quote: QSO at z=6.13 Figure fig:qso_spec shows the ALMA OH spectrum extracted from the inner 3 kpc region of . The rest-frame velocity is set based on a precise redshift measurement derived from the detection of the \ line by . We observe a tentative absorption feature blueshifted from the systemic velocity by 150 km s ^ 1 that could indicate the presence of a molecular outflow. The significance of the detection ---based on the integrated flux inside a single Gaussian fit--- is 3 , and the median ( v_ 50 ) and v_ 84 velocities are 154 km s ^ 1 and 314 km s ^ 1 , respectively.
Figure fig:comp shows the median velocities and equivalent widths of the \ profiles measured in IRAS F13352+6402, IRAS F20036-1547, IRAS F10091+4704, and QSO J131911+095051.

Based on the empirical relation between M _ out,mol , EW ^ v<-200 _ OH , and L_ FIR (Appendix app:ew ) we determine a molecular mass outflow rate for \ of M _ out,mol 200 M_ yr ^ 1 . This value is only half the star formation rate of the QSO ( SFR =375 M_ yr ^ 1 if we assume that only 10 of L_ FIR is associated with the starburst), which is in stark contrast with the typical molecular mass loading factor of 1-10 measured in local (U)LIRGs e.g., rhc_veilleux13,rhc_gonzalez-alfonso17,rhc_rhc19b . The modest mass outflow rate in \ is, however, comparable to the low values found in the stacking analysis of the \ spectra of z 5-7 QSOs by ( M _ out,mol 100-200 M_ yr ^ 1 ) and .

sources.summary: The paper reports J1319+0950 as a UKIDSS z~6 quasar with strong 250 GHz dust continuum, radio continuum, CO(6-5) line emission, and high FIR luminosity.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The radio emission appears unresolved with a peak at 13^h19^m11.30^s , 09^ 50'51.8'' , i.e. within 0.4'' of the optical quasar position.

quotes.quote: J1319+0950 The source was discovered in the UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey (UKIDSS) with m_ 1450 =19.65 , which lies in the typical magnitude range of the optically bright z 6 quasars selected from the SDSS main survey (Mortlock et al. 2008). We detect the 250 GHz dust continuum emission from this object with a flux density of S_ 250 GHz =4.20 0.65 mJy , which is the third-strongest 250 GHz detection among the known z 6 quasars. The 1.4 GHz radio continuum is also detected from our VLA observation, with S_ 1.4 GHz =64 17 Jy (left panel of Figure 1). The radio emission appears unresolved with a peak at 13^h19^m11.30^s , 09^ 50'51.8'' , i.e. within 0.4'' of the optical quasar position. We also see a 4 peak ( S_ 1.4 GHz =68 17 Jy ) at 13^h19^m11.65^s , 09^ 50'53.2'' on the radio map, which is 5.6'' away from the quasar.
The line is detected at 4.8 on the velocity-integrated map with a line flux of 0.43 0.09 Jy km s^ 1 , 0.9'' away from (i.e., consistent with) the quasar position (the upper panel of Figure 2).
The FIR luminosity of the strongest MAMBO detection, J1319+0950, is 1.0 10^ 13 L_ , making it one of the most FIR-luminous objects among the z 6 quasar sample.

sources.summary: ALMA imaging of J1319+0950 detects [C II] and continuum emission, resolves the line-emitting source, and shows a clear velocity gradient with channel-position shifts.

quotes.quote: J1319+0950 . This quasar was discovered in the UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey (UKIDSS) and has m_ 1450 =19.65 mortlock09 . The [ C II ] line emission is imaged by ALMA with a synthesized beam size of 0.69'' 0.49'' . The 2-D Gaussian fit suggests line emission with a deconvolved source size of (0.57'' 0.07'') (0.32'' 0.15'') oriented at PA=28^ 18^ .

The line velocity map shows a clear gradient from the Southwest to the Northeast. We plot the [ C II ] line channel map of this object in Figure 3. The map shows a clear position shift of the [ C II ] line peak (by 0.4'' ) from the +222 km s^ 1 channel to the 200 km s^ 1 channel.

sources.summary: The paper reports excellent-weather observations of J1319+0950, uses published SED-fit values for its infrared luminosity, and includes it among high-resolution quasar hosts used for gas estimates.

quotes.quote: Observations of J1319+0950 were performed in excellent weather conditions (precipitable water vapour <2 mm, system temperature T_ sys =55-70 K) and used on the calibrators 3C273 (bandpass), MWC349 (flux), and 1307+121 (phase, amplitude, pointing, focus). The final cubes consists of 8310 visibilities (2.88 h on source, 9-antenna equivalent).
For the quasars J0338+0021, J2054--0005, and J1319+0950, we instead adopt the values published in wang13 and leipski14 , based on the fits of their Spectral Energy Distributions.

Along the same lines, one could adopt the method proposed by sommovigo21 to infer _ CII and T_ d based on the surface brightness of for those quasars in our sample that have already been observed at high angular resolution in PJ036+03, J1048--0109, J1319+0950, J2054--0005, and PJ359--06; see venemans20 . The extremely high SFR densities reached in these sources lead to very low _ CII 1 values and correspondingly to slightly higher dust temperatures, T_ d =40--100 K.

sources.summary: The paper summarizes J1319+0950 as an optically bright z~6 QSO with CO gas, [CII] dynamical mass, dust SED measurements, and tentative molecular outflow evidence.

quotes.quote: ULAS J131911.29+095051.4 (hereafter J1319+0950) at z=6.133 was discovered in the UKIRT Infrared Deep Sky Survey (UKIDSS) with m_ 1450 = 19.65 , which lies in the typical magnitude range of the optically bright z 6 quasars selected from the SDSS main survey mortlock2008 . wang2011 detected the CO(6-5) emission line and its underlying continuum, deriving a gas mass of M_ gas =1.5 10^ 10 \ M_ . Later, wang2013 analysed the [ CII ] line emission and underlying continuum, deriving a dynamical mass of M_ dyn =12.5 10^ 10 \ M_ (also confirmed by shao2017 ). shao2017 estimated a BH mass M_ BH = (2.7 0.6) 10^9 \ M_ from the MgII line, which contributes 2 of the dynamical mass of the system. carniani2019 performed a detailed study of the cold dust SED of J1319+1950 deriving a dust mass of (M_ dust /M_ )=8.8 0.2 and a dust temperature of T_ dust =66^ +15 _ 10 K.

gmw_00000839.name: J0129 0035
gmw_00000839.overview: J0129 0035 is a faint optical quasar at a redshift of approximately 5.78 [arXiv:1107.5065, arXiv:1302.4154]. Its host galaxy contains bright dust continuum and CO line emission [arXiv:1107.5065, arXiv:1302.4154], with resolved [C II] observations revealing a rotating disk structure combined with turbulent gas clumps [arXiv:1904.07749].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0129 0035 as the faintest SDSS z~6 quasar, with detected 250 GHz dust continuum, CO(6-5) emission, a CO redshift, and underlying dust continuum.

quotes.quote: J0129 0035 This is the faintest of the SDSS z 6 quasars, with m_ 1450 =22.16 (Jiang et al. 2009). We detect 250 GHz dust continuum from the quasar host galaxy, with a flux density of S_ 250 GHz =2.37 0.49 mJy , comparable to the flux densities of the optically bright z 6 quasars with 250 GHz detections. We detected the CO (6-5) line in this source with a flux of 0.37 0.07 Jy km s^ 1 , i.e., at 5.3 , integrated over a velocity range of 380 km s^ 1 (the middle panel of Figure 2). The line width fitted with a single Gaussian is 283 87 km s^ 1 , with a line peak of 1.2 0.3 mJy. The host galaxy redshift measured with the CO line is z=5.7794 0.0008 , which is in good agreement with the optical redshift of z_ Ly =5.78 0.03 measured with Ly (Jiang et al. 2009).
The underlying dust continuum is also detected with a flux density of S_ 102 GHz =0.14 0.04 mJy .

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0129 0035 as a faint optical z~6 quasar with bright millimeter and CO emission, resolved [C II] and continuum sizes, and a velocity gradient.

quotes.quote: J0129 0035 . This quasar was discovered in SDSS Stripe 82 with m_ 1450 =22.16 jiang09 . It is the faintest optical source among the MAMBO-detected z 6 quasars, but previous millimeter observations found bright 250 GHz dust continuum and CO (6-5) line emission from this object wang11a . The [ C II ] line was observed at 0.57'' 0.49'' resolution.

The deconvolved source size is estimated to be (0.41'' 0.06'') (0.23'' 0.12'') with PA=12^ 22^ . A velocity gradient is found from the Southeast to the Northwest. A 2-D Gaussian fit to the continuum image gives a 287 GHz flux density of 2.57 0.06 mJy , with a deconvolved continuum source size of (0.23'' 0.04'') (0.14'' 0.07'') at PA=115^ 154^ .

sources.summary: The paper resolves SDSS J0129-0035 in [C II] and dust, finding rotating but turbulent gas, extended [C II] emission, and a near-local black hole-to-host mass relation.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: For SDSS J0129-0035, the quasar coordinates are RA=01 ^ h 29 ^ m 58.524 ^ s , and DEC=-00 ^d 35 ' 39.77 '' with an uncertainty of 149 mas in RA and 114 mas in DEC.

quotes.quote: We fit the velocity map of the [ CII ] emission from SDSS J0129-0035 with a rotating disk model. The result suggests a face-on system with an inclination angle of 16 20^ and constrains the lower limit of the host galaxy dynamical mass to be 2.6 10^ 10 M_ within the [ CII ] emitting region. It is likely that SDSS J0129-0035, as well as other young quasars with super massive black hole masses on order of 10^ 7 M_ to 10^ 8 M_ , falls close to the black hole and host galaxy mass relation defined by local galaxies.

The velocity field and PV diagrams of SDSS J0129-0035 reveal a combination of rotation with turbulent gas clumps; We detect clear velocity gradients in the velocity map. We also see features with velocities offsets of -190 and +50 km s^ 1 at the outer part of the [ C II ] emitting region and gas clumps with velocity offsets of 200 km s^ 1 within the central 0.2 '' region, suggesting other kinematic activities, such as inflow or outflows.

gmw_00000840.name: J0203+0012
gmw_00000840.overview: J0203+0012 is a high-redshift broad-absorption-line quasar [arXiv:1503.04214, arXiv:1107.5065] with a redshift estimated between z=5.72 and z=5.86 [arXiv:astro-ph/0612162, arXiv:1107.5065]. Although initially classified as radio-loud, more reliable infrared-based measurements suggest it is a radio-quiet source [arXiv:1503.04214] that does not reside in a particularly dense environment [arXiv:1503.04214].

sources.summary: The paper reassesses J0203+0012, arguing that infrared-based luminosity makes this broad-absorption-line quasar radio-quiet rather than radio-loud.

quotes.quote: J0203+0012: A Radio-Loud Quasar? The quasar J0203+0012 at z=5.72 was classified as radio-loud quasar by . They derived the rest-frame 4400 \ flux density from the 1450 \ magnitude. As discussed previously, these large extrapolations can carry large uncertainties and can be critical for the classification and derived parameters for specific objects. This is the case for J0203+0012, a broad-absorption line quasar mortlock2009 whose m_ 1450 could have been underestimated, resulting in a low optical luminosity (see also its spectral energy distribution in Figure 14 of leipski2014 ).

This quasar has a radio-loudness parameter R=4.3 0.5 or R=12.1 3.2 depending if the rest-frame 4400 \ flux density is extrapolated from the IRAC 3.6 m photometry or from m_ 1450 , respectively (see Figure fig:radio ). It is clear that the difference is dramatic and that by using the m_ 1450 proxy this quasar would be classified (barely) as radio-loud. We argue that the value of L_ 4400 obtained from the observed 3.6 m photometry is more reliable for z>5.5 quasars since it relies less on extrapolation (for this particular case, the extrapolation is less by approximately a factor of three). Also, while radio-loud AGN are typically located in dense environments e.g., venemans2007b,hatch2014 , we found that J0203+0012 does not live in a particularly dense region but rather comparable with what is expected in blank fields banados2013 .

sources.summary: The paper reports ULAS J020332.38+001229.2 as a luminous z=5.86 quasar whose FORS2 spectrum shows broad Ly emission, a continuum break, and a fitted redshift of z=5.864.

quotes.quote: Our analysis of 106 deg ^2 of sky from Data Release 1 (DR1) has resulted in the discovery of ULAS J020332.38+001229.2, a luminous ( J_ AB =20.0 , J_ Vega =19.1 , M_ 1450 =-26.2 ) quasar at z=5.86 .

Total exposure times were 1200 s for ULAS J020332.38+001229.2 (hereafter ULAS J0203+0012) and 2400 s for ULAS J022905.11+005506.5. Fig. \ spectrum shows the spectrum of ULAS J0203+0012. The spectrum is characterized by a broad, asymmetric emission line and a continuum break around 8350 . We interpret these features as the Ly emission line and the continuum break resulting from Ly forest absorption at a redshift of z 5.86 .

We determined the redshift of the quasar by fitting a Gaussian to the red part of the Ly line. This gives a redshift of z= 5.864 0.003. The peak of the emission is at 8342 \ giving a redshift of z=5.862 .

sources.summary: The paper describes J0203+0012 as a BAL quasar with detected radio and 250 GHz dust continuum, but no detected CO(6-5) line or 3 mm continuum.

quotes.quote: J0203+0012 This object is a broad absorption line (BAL) quasar discovered by both the SDSS southern survey (Jiang et al. 2008) and the UKIDSS survey (Venemans et al. 2007; Mortlock et al. 2008) with m_ 1450 =20.97 . The redshift measured with the quasar N V line emission is z=5.72 (Mortlock et al. 2008). Wang et al. (2008b) detected the 1.4 GHz radio continuum with S_ 1.4 GHz =195 22 Jy (Wang et al. 2008b). Our MAMBO observation yields a 250 GHz dust continuum flux density of S_ 250GHz =1.85 0.46 mJy . We have searched for the CO (6-5) line emission from this object over a wide redshift range, from z=5.60 to 5.84, but did not detect it in 14 hours of on-source time.
The dust continuum at the CO (6-5) line frequency is also undetected with a continuum sensitivity of 0.04 mJy beam^ 1 and a 3 upper limit of 0.12 mJy.

sources.summary: The paper gives SDSS-J0203+0012 a low host stellar-mass upper limit relative to its large C IV black-hole mass, but downweights the tension because the BAL quasar has outflow-affected C IV.

quotes.quote: SDSS-J0203+0012, however, has a stellar mass of M_ <1.89 10^ 11 M_ , lower than expected by the local relation, given its extremely large black hole mass of M_ BH =5.2 10^ 10 M_ Table tab_targets , Shen2019 . However, we note that this black hole mass is determined by the C IV line, as the more robust Mg II line is not covered by ground-based observations. SDSS-J0203+0012 is a broad absorption line (BAL) quasar mortlock_2009 and so the dynamics probed by the C IV line are likely affected by the outflows. Hence we place no significance on our black hole--stellar mass relation limit for this object.

gmw_00000841.name: RBS 0970
gmw_00000841.overview: RBS 0970 is a point-like radio source and active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.124 [arXiv:1404.3583]. Optically identified as a BL Lac object and blazar, it has been detected as a source of very high energy gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1404.3583].

sources.summary: The letter reports RBS 0970 as a BL Lac/blazar detected by Fermi LAT at very high energies, with clustered VHE photons, spectral variability, and follow-up potential.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: centered on the Second Fermi Source Catalogue (2FGL; ) position of RBS 0970, ( _ J2000 , _ J2000 =170 ^ .2, 42 ^ .2039)

quotes.quote: In original form 2014 January 31 firstpage - lastpage 2014 In this letter I report the Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT) detection of Very High Energy (VHE; E>100 GeV) ray emission from the BL Lac object RBS 0970. 5.3 years of LAT observations revealed the presence of 3 VHE photon events within 0 ^ .1 of RBS 0970, with a subsequent unbinned likelihood analysis finding RBS 0970 to be a source of VHE photons at the 6.5 level of confidence. The 1 GeV flux, binned in monthly periods, did not indicate any flux brightening of RBS 0970 accompanying the emission of the VHE photons.

RBS 0970 is a point-like radio source, with a redshift of z =0.124 ( gommi ). Detected by successive X-ray surveys using the EINSTEIN, ROSAT and BEPPO-SAX satellites ( einstein , rosat , beppo ), RBS 0970 has been optically identified as a BL Lac object with SDSS observations ( sdss ). As a member of the blazar sub-class of AGN, it is no surprise that RBS 0970 is present in both the 1 and 2 year Fermi LAT AGN catalogues ( 1agn ; acker3 ).
As such, this analysis represents the discovery of RBS 0970 as a source of VHE rays.

gmw_00000842.name: RXC J2248.7-4431-ID3
gmw_00000842.overview: RXC J2248.7-4431-ID3 is a gravitationally lensed, low-mass galaxy at a redshift of z=6.11 [arXiv:1611.07125, arXiv:1901.11045]. It features highly ionized carbon and oxygen emission lines [arXiv:1611.07125, arXiv:1901.11045] that indicate a hard ionizing spectrum driven by low-metallicity stars rather than an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1611.07125, arXiv:1901.11045].

sources.summary: The paper reports FIRE spectroscopy of the z=6.11 lensed low-mass galaxy, detecting CIV and OIII], finding no HeII, and interpreting the line ratios as a hard stellar ionizing spectrum.

quotes.quote: We present the Magellan/FIRE detection of highly-ionized CIV 1550 and OIII ] 1666 in a deep infrared spectrum of the z=6.11 gravitationally lensed low-mass galaxy RXC J2248.7-4431-ID3, which has previously-known Ly . No corresponding emission is detected at the expected location of HeII 1640. The upper limit on HeII paired with detection of OIII ] and CIV constrains possible ionization scenarios. Production of CIV and OIII ] requires ionizing photons of 2.5-3.5 Ryd, but once in that state their multiplet emission is powered by collisional excitation at lower energies ( 0.5 Ryd). As a pure recombination line, HeII emission is powered by 4 Ryd ionizing photons.

The data therefore require a spectrum with significant power at 3.5 Ryd but a rapid drop toward 4.0 Ryd. This hard spectrum with a steep drop is characteristic of low-metallicity stellar populations, and less consistent with soft AGN excitation, which features more 4 Ryd photons and hence higher HeII flux.

RXJ2248-ID3 adds to the growing handful of reionization-era galaxies with UV emission line ratios distinct from the general z=2-3 population, in a way that suggests hard ionizing spectra that do not necessarily originate in AGN.

sources.summary: The paper observes a multiply imaged z=6.11 galaxy in RXJ2248, detecting C IV emission in one image and interpreting its highly ionized lines as evidence for a hard non-AGN spectrum.

quotes.quote: As well as \ candidates we also targeted 3 images of a multiply-imaged z=6.11 galaxy in RXJ2248 to observe 1548,1551 emission. This multiple-image system has been detected with \ emission by Boone2013,Balestra2013,Monna2014,Karman2015,Schmidt2016 and Mainali2017 . Detections of \ and ] 1666 emission in one of the images were also presented by Mainali2017 , and \ detections and limits in all of the images by Schmidt2017a . The presence of these highly ionised lines and lack of observed 1640 indicate this object has a hard ionizing spectrum, but unlikely to be dominated by an AGN.

In Figure  fig:CIV we show our GLASS and KMOS spectra for the brightest image E. An emission line is seen in the KMOS data at the same wavelength as Mainali2017 , and appears to be spatially compact with size approximately equal to our seeing ( 0 6 ).

gmw_00000843.name: NGC 2146
gmw_00000843.overview: NGC 2146 is a nearby barred spiral [arXiv:2007.14348, arXiv:2012.10054] and luminous infrared starburst galaxy [arXiv:2009.07293, arXiv:1305.2923] featuring a warped disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0610114] and strong dust lanes [arXiv:1305.2923]. Its intense starburst activity is driven by a merger or tidal interaction [arXiv:1207.1448, arXiv:1710.03149], which has produced extended tidal streams [arXiv:1207.1448, arXiv:2011.02176] and strong stellar winds [arXiv:1305.2923].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2146 as a data-supplemented KINGFISH galaxy with one of the largest dust/stellar ratios, warm dust, peculiar morphology, and transition-galaxy characteristics.

quotes.quote: For the two galaxies that were neither in SINGS nor LVL, IC 342 and NGC 2146, we obtained flux densities from various sources. We obtained UV magnitudes from GALEX (using the values quoted in Gil de Paz et al. \ 2007 for NGC 2146), and converted them to fluxes using the calibration in Morrissey et al. \ (2007). We obtained optical fluxes from Buta McCall (1999) and Marcum et al. \ (2001), respectively. We obtained 2MASS fluxes from Jarrett et al. \ (2003), using the calibration in Cohen et al. \ (2003). We measured Spitzer fluxes from their images, and we add the IRAS 100 m flux for NGC 2146.

The three galaxies with the largest dust/stellar flux ratios are NGC 1482, NGC 2146, and NGC 2798, which are all starbursting early-type spirals. NGC 1482 also has evidence of superwind outflows (Hota Saikia 2005), while NGC 2146 and NGC 2798 have peculiar morphologies, possibly indicative of interactions or mergers (but see Greve et al. \ 2006 on NGC 2146). The two galaxies with the smallest ratios are NGC 1404 and NGC 584, which are both massive ellipticals (or E/S0) with very little FIR emission; NGC 1404 is also experiencing ram-pressure stripping as it falls through the Fornax cluster (Machacek et al. \ 2005). NGC 1404's f_ dust /f_ ( 0.006 ) should be considered an upper limit, because its FIR and submm fluxes appear to be due to a background source (Dale et al., in prep.), and it is an indication of the most stellar-dominated a galaxy's SED can be. In Table table1 , we show the dust/stellar flux ratios of the KINGFISH galaxies. The near-IR morphologies, TIR luminosities, dust masses, stellar masses, and star formation rates are also listed in the table, and are discussed below. The columns are: galaxy name; morphological type, from Kennicutt et al. \ (2003); redshift-independent distance (see Kennicutt et al., in prep.); dust/stellar flux ratio (Eqn. ratio ); TIR luminosity, using Draine Li (2007) calibration; dust temperature, estimated from modified blackbody fit to far-IR SED; dust mass, converted from dust temperatures using Li Draine (2001) 500 m mass absorption coefficient; stellar mass, using Zibetti et al. \ (2009) calibration; star formation rate (SFR), from FUV and TIR luminosities, and from H and 24 m luminosities. The mass and SFR errors are underestimates: they include only formal errors from the fluxes, not systematic errors. ^ a Morphology obtained from Buta et al. \ (2010), and was different than that listed in Kennicutt et al. \ (2003). ^ b M_ dust estimated from MIPS fluxes only, without longer wavelength fluxes. ^ Stellar mass-to-light ratios estimated from g-i and i-H colors; the others are estimated from B-V and V-H colors (see Zibetti et al. \ 2009). ^ d Far-UV flux extrapolated from longer wavelengths. ^ e H flux obtained from Kennicutt et al. \ (2008). ^ f H flux obtained from Marcum et al. \ (2001). Total Infrared Luminosity Estimating L_ TIR We begin by analyzing the total infrared (TIR) luminosity of the galaxies in our sample. The TIR luminosity is a useful quantity because it can be directly inferred from the IR fluxes, and because it can be used as a proxy for the obscured star formation as well as the temperature of dust grains (e.g., Dale Helou 2002, Draine Li 2007). We follow Draine Li (2007), and use the 8, 24, 70, 160 m data from IRAC and MIPS to estimate the TIR luminosity: L_ TIR = 0.95 L_ _ 7.9 + 1.15 L_ _ 24 + L_ _ 71 + L_ _ 160 We have also tested the Dale Helou (2002) formula, which uses only the MIPS bands, and have obtained very similar results. (We have chosen not to include the SPIRE bands in Eqn. DL07LTIR , because a calibration of L_ TIR with these bands has not yet been developed and tested.) Estimates of L_ TIR are designed to encompass all of the emission from PAH particles, very small grains, and large grains, the proportions of which depend on the starlight density distribution and the relative abundances of the grain populations (Draine Li 2007; Compi e gne et al. \ 2011). Therefore, it is useful to compare L_ TIR and the dust/stellar flux ratio, in order to analyze the emission from dust grains vis- a vis stellar emission. Results The correlation between L_ TIR and the dust/stellar flux ratio of the KINGFISH galaxies is shown in Figure TIRcorr . As mentioned in Section dusttostellar , the total IR luminosity is very closely related to the quantity f_ dust . Plotting f_ dust D^2 versus f_ dust /f_ yields a result very similar to that shown in the figure. In Figure TIRcorr a, the galaxies are also labeled by their morphologies, such that we distinguish galaxies that are classified as E and S0, Sa to Scd, and Sd and later-type. The spiral galaxies NGC 2146 and NGC 1097 have the largest TIR luminosities, while the dwarf galaxies DDO 154, DDO 165, and M81 Dwarf B have the faintest luminosities. The late-type spiral galaxies NGC 598 and NGC 7793 have almost exactly the same f_ dust /f_ and L_ TIR ; as noted by Smith et al. \ (1984), these galaxies have similar photometric and kinematic properties, but different spiral arm structures. Many of the spirals tend to be found in the locus of f_ dust /f_ just below unity and L_ TIR >10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 in the figure. Most of the galaxies with small dust/stellar flux ratios and large TIR luminosities are early-types, although this may be partly due to a selection effect, as these galaxies were selected to be detectable in the IR (Kennicutt et al. \ 2003). In contrast, those with small f_ dust /f_ and small L_ TIR are dwarfs and irregulars. Here and in terms of other galaxy properties, some of the earlier-type spirals follow the trends of the E and S0 galaxies, while others have properties more similar to other spiral galaxies. The trend in Figure TIRcorr is more complicated than a simple morphological distinction, however, as some early-types have relatively large dust/stellar flux ratios ( f_ dust /f_ 1 ); as we will show later, these galaxies also tend to have larger specific star formation rates and dust temperatures. Note that Dale et al. \ (2009) have a somewhat similar plot (their Fig. 3), in which L_ TIR /L_B is plotted on the horizontal axis, rather than the dust/stellar flux ratio. Their sample is obtained from the LVL survey, which was designed to be nearly volume-limited. As a result, it is dominated by faint dwarf galaxies, and some of these fill part of the parameter space in the lower right of the figure, with low L_ TIR and slightly higher dust/stellar flux ratios than the dwarf galaxies in KINGFISH. In comparison, KINGFISH is incomplete and has relatively few dwarf galaxies, so the survey mostly covers the high-TIR part of the parameter space. Lastly, Soifer et al. \ (1989) also performed a similar analysis, plotting infrared/visible flux versus FIR luminosity for a sample of IR-bright galaxies in IRAS. They obtained a single trend albeit with significant dispersion, but most of the dwarf galaxies in KINGFISH would likely not have met their selection criteria. We summarize the morphology dependence of TIR luminosity and other galaxy properties analyzed in this section in Table table2 , which lists the means and standard deviations of these properties as a function of Hubble type. These could be useful as a local benchmark for comparisons with high-redshift studies, such as studies of submillimeter galaxies (e.g., Santini et al. \ 2010). KINGFISH galaxy properties: mean bootstrap error standard deviation. There are 17 dwarf and irregular galaxies (Sd and later), 35 spirals (Sa to Scd), and 10 early-types (E and S0). f_ dust /f_ , M_ dust /M_ , L_ TIR , T_ dust , and SFR are discussed in Sections dusttostellar , masses , TIRlum , FIRcolors , and SFRsec , respectively. SSFR refers to specific star formation rate, SFR/M_ ; the quantities in these two columns include galaxies with H fluxes from Kennicutt et al. \ (2008) and galaxies with extrapolated FUV fluxes (see Section dusttostellar ). As mentioned above, approximately half of the KINGFISH galaxies have strong bars, and it is possible that the presence of a bar affects emission by dust and stars in the central region of a galaxy. We find that the mean of the log dust/stellar flux ratio is 0.57 0.10 for weakly barred galaxies (no bar or SA) and 0.48 0.09 for strongly barred galaxies (SAB or SB). On the other hand, for the total infrared luminosity, the means are log L_ TIR =42.9 0.2 and 43.3 0.2 for weakly and strongly barred galaxies, respectively. This is consistent with the observation that most IR-selected starburst galaxies are barred (e.g., Hunt Malkan 1999). Nevertheless, the bar fraction has also been observed to be higher among galaxies with higher optical luminosities, because these galaxies became dynamically cool and sufficiently massive to host bars earlier than fainter galaxies (Sheth et al. \ 2008). Thus one might expect barred galaxies to have brighter stellar and infrared luminosities than weakly barred ones. Our data in fact bear this out: the mean stellar and dust luminosities, which we quantify as f_ (4 D^2) and f_ dust (4 D^2) , are both higher in barred galaxies by 0.43 dex, the same factor as log L_ TIR . Therefore, it is simply the case that strongly barred galaxies in KINGFISH are more luminous than weakly barred ones, and this effect cancels in the dust/stellar flux ratios. In Figure TIRcorr b, we show the metallicity dependence of the relation between the TIR luminosity and dust/stellar flux ratio. Firstly, it can be seen that the metallicity dependence and morphology dependence are similar: dwarf galaxies tend to be relatively metal-poor, spiral galaxies tend to have intermediate metallicities, and early-type galaxies tend to be metal-rich. Nonetheless, it is not a one-to-one relationship: some early-type spirals with large L_ TIR are also metal-rich (such as NGC 1097), while some intermediate-metallicity galaxies are early-types with relatively large f_ dust /f_ (such as NGC 855). In general, from the morphological and metallicity dependence of L_ TIR and f_ dust /f_ , we can tentatively infer an evolutionary sequence from the figure, such that as a typical late-type galaxy grows and becomes more luminous, it becomes more metal enriched and has a larger dust fraction; this is also accompanied by more emission by stars, a larger metallicity, and a growing stellar mass (either by star formation or a merger), as the galaxy becomes an early-type. For the early-types, either stellar mass growth outweighs dust production, because the dense molecular clouds in which dust is typically accreted have dissolved, or a substantial amount of dust grains are ejected or destroyed, such as by supernovae shockwaves and thermal `sputtering' (e.g., Draine Salpeter 1979; Dwek 1998; Pipino et al. \ 2011). This interpretation of Figure TIRcorr is merely speculative, however, and assumes that typical present-day late-types resemble past stages of present-day early-types; that is, it assumes that the growth of galaxy disks precedes that of bulges (e.g., Bournaud et al. \ 2009; Ceverino et al. \ 2010), although there is evidence that this assumption may be too simplistic (e.g., MacArthur et al. \ 2009; Bundy et al. \ 2010). In contrast, the dwarf and irregular galaxies have lower TIR luminosities (most have L_ TIR <10^ 42.5 erg s^ 1 ) and relatively little emission from dust (usually f_ dust /f_ <0.3 ). They appear to be a distinctly different galaxy population, exhibiting different properties than typical late-type spirals and inhabiting different environments (e.g., Leroy et al. \ 2008; Gavazzi et al. \ 2010). In any case, although general trends are apparent in Figure TIRcorr , there are plenty of exceptions and variation within the KINGFISH sample. Note that the sample was selected not to have strong AGN, defined as an AGN that dominates substantial portions of a galaxy's spectrum, so it is unlikely that AGN contribute much to this variation. Dust and Stellar Mass Next, in order to add to this picture of galaxy evolution, we can use the infrared SEDs of the galaxies in our sample to estimate dust and stellar masses. These are more physical quantities than fluxes, but they require some model assumptions, and hence have additional systematic uncertainties. Estimating T_ dust and M_ dust We estimate dust masses using dust temperatures, which are determined from simple fits to the FIR SED (e.g., Hildebrand 1983). We use the MIPS and SPIRE FIR and submm ( 70-500 m) flux densities and perform fits using a single temperature blackbody modified by an emissivity law proportional to ^ . Our approach is similar to that of Engelbracht et al. \ (2010) and Gordon et al. \ (2010), who also applied this method to galaxies with MIPS and SPIRE data. The use of a single temperature fit for whole galaxies essentially yields an average dust temperature; in practice, most galaxies have multiple dust components at different temperatures, such as in photo-dissociation regions and in the diffuse ISM, although one component might dominate. We assume =1.5 , which is consistent with recent observational constraints at far-IR and submm wavelengths (e.g., Dunne Eales 2001; Paradis et al. \ 2009; Gordon et al. \ 2010). In any case, the resulting dust temperatures are slightly dependent upon the assumed emissivity, and it is in a systematic way, such that =2 would yield slightly lower temperatures by a few degrees for the whole sample (e.g., Bendo et al. \ 2003). We also do not attempt to account for the `submm excess' inferred by some authors which may be due to a very cold dust component or to a wavelength dependent emissivity law (e.g., Galametz et al. \ 2009; Gordon et al. \ 2010). We infer the uncertainties of the temperatures with a Monte Carlo analysis that includes the flux errors. Most of the resulting temperatures range from 20-35 K, with NGC 1512 having the coldest temperature ( 21 K), and NGC 1377 and NGC 2146 having exceptionally warm ones ( 43 K and 37 K, respectively).

sources.summary: The paper reports a ring-like cluster complex in the starburst galaxy NGC 2146, locating it in a tidal stream and using HST imaging to study triggered star formation.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a ring-like cluster complex in the starburst galaxy NGC 2146. The Ruby Ring, so named due to its appearance, shows a clear ring-like distribution of star clusters around a central object. It is located in one of the tidal streams which surround the galaxy. NGC 2146 is part of the Snapshot Hubble U band Cluster Survey ( SHUCS ).

The WFC3/F336W data has added critical information to the available archival Hubble Space Telescope imaging set of NGC 2146, allowing us to determine ages, masses, and extinctions of the clusters in the Ruby Ring. We find evidence of a spatial and temporal correlation between the central cluster and the clusters in the ring. The latter are about 4 Myr younger than the central cluster. This result is supported by the H emission which is strongly coincident with the ring, and weaker at the position of the central cluster.

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 2146 with a simulated star-forming galaxy, reproducing its flat high-frequency spectrum with thermal free-free emission and predicting low-frequency central flattening.

quotes.quote: Thermal and non-thermal radio emission Figures fig: radio-spectra NGC 253 and M82 and fig: radio-spectrum NGC2146 show the radio spectra of the three star-forming galaxies NGC 253, M82 and NGC 2146. We compare observations by , 2013Adebahr_M82 and 2018Klein to radio spectra derived from our simulated galaxies that resemble the observed ones in terms of their SFRs and radio luminosities, respectively (see Table Table-Galaxies ). We show the observed and simulated total spectra (top panels) and the spectra from the central regions (middle panels).

Similarly to NGC 253 and M82, the observed spectrum of NGC 2146 is flatter than the radio synchrotron emission of our simulated galaxy. However, adding a thermal free-free component enables us to reproduce the flat observed spectrum at frequencies above 10 GHz. In the case of NGC 2146, there are no published data of the central region. We chose to show the central spectrum of NGC 2146 from a region with radius of 0.3 kpc, where we find a pronounced flattening of the central spectrum at low frequencies due to free-fee absorption.
We determine the contribution of the thermal free-free emission to the total luminosity at 1.4 GHz to 24.7, 6.3 and 5.7 per cent for NGC 253, M82 and NGC 2146, respectively.

sources.summary: NGC 2146 is described as a nearby barred spiral LIRG with nuclear-starburst-dominated star formation, ISO far-infrared line detections, and consistent optical/far-infrared N/O results.

quotes.quote: NGC 2146 is a barred spiral galaxy, and one of the nearest luminous infrared galaxies. The star formation rate is estimated to be 7.9 M_ /yr , dominated by the nuclear starburst. It is in the GOALS sample. NGC 2146 has Infrared Space Observatory (ISO) detection of the [ N iii ] line as well as both of the [ O iii ] lines. Besides, it is the only source not classified as ``extended,, in and with all three lines detected, plus it has ample optical and mid-infrared data available to aide our N/O measurement.

While the star formation activities in NGC 2146 are extended over 2' scales 1991ApJ...373..423S , the ISO beam is uniform at about 70" for these lines and therefore provides good measurements of the inner 4.9 kpc region of this galaxy.
For NGC 4214, NGC 2146 and M83 nucleus, the far-IR derived and optical derived N/O abundance ratios agree within the 1 error.

sources.summary: NGC 2146 is modeled as a GeV-detected star-forming galaxy; its luminosity is a slightly poor fit unless calorimetric assumptions or another emission component are invoked, affecting CTA prospects.

quotes.quote: Although NGC 2146 shows a slightly bad fit, our model is in good agreement with observations as a whole, and performs better than the simpler assumptions such as L_ (0.1--800 GeV) (the calorimetric limit) or L_ (0.1--800 GeV) M_ gas (intended to be the escape limit, though M_ gas should be ISM density in reality). This is because we estimate the fraction of confined protons by considering more detailed physical conditions than simple models. The data point for M33, which was not used to determine C , is also shown here, and the luminosity predicted by our model is in reasonable agreement with the newly reported observed luminosity. The observed luminosity of NGC 2146 is explained if we assume the calorimetric limit, which indicates that the emission region of this galaxy might be much smaller than our assumption, or perhaps there is another emission component.
The spectra of the top six galaxies (NGC 5236, M33, NGC 6946, IC 342, NGC 2146, and NGC 7331) are shown in Figure fig:spectrum_examples .
We note that the chance of TeV gamma-ray detection for NGC 2146 is enhanced if the TeV flux is higher than that of our model by the same factor as the discrepancy at GeV energy range.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2146 as a nearby starburst with a molecular ring, hot outflow, dust-obscured nucleus, and compact radio sources interpreted as young H II regions and fewer supernova remnants than M 82.

quotes.quote: NGC 2146 is a starburst galaxy located at a distance of 14.5 Mpc (1 70 pc). Its infrared luminosity measured with IRAS at 60 m and 100 m is 6.6 10^ 10 L_ . This value and the large 25 m to 60 m flux ratio place the object in the lower part of the IR luminosity/spectral index plane populated by superluminous galaxies without active nuclei (Hutchings et al. ). NGC 2146 has a central molecular ring (Jackson Ho , Young et al. ), and an outflow of hot gas along the minor axis driven by supernova explosions and stellar winds in the starburst region (Armus et al. , Della Ceca et al. ).

When compared with M 82, the galaxy NGC 2146 lacks however a large number of supernova remnants. We suggest that NGC 2146 is experiencing a burst of star formation stronger than that in M 82, but being in a younger phase.

sources.summary: The paper removes NGC 2146 because its coincident gamma-ray source is associated with a debated BL Lac object, then tests that treating it as gamma-ray bright does not change results.

quotes.quote: Third, we remove the system NGC 2146 from our analysis, noting that the system is coincident with a ray source (3FGL J0707.0+7741). However, in the Fermi 3FGL catalog this source is associated with the nearby BL Lac object NVSS J070651+774137 Acero:2015hja , though the association is debated Tang:2014dia . We also produce an analysis which includes the systems M31 and NGC 2146 as ray bright point sources, finding that they do not significantly affect the results shown here.

We show the best-fit parameters for our default scenario where the systems NGC 224 and NGC 2146 are ignored (left), and in an alternative scenario where NGC 224 is modeled as a point-source and NGC 2146 is modeled under the assumption that the nearby blazar produces no ray emission (right). We find the results in each case to be similar and note three key results.

sources.summary: The paper presents new CanariCam images of NGC 2146, showing diffuse MIR emission along a southeast-northwest band aligned with a dust lane, but excluding it from the AGN sample because no clear nucleus was detected.

quotes.quote: Four galaxies were observed with CanariCam: NGC 931, NGC 1194, NGC 2146 and NGC 2273. The images in the filters PAH2 - 11.3 and Si5 - 11.6 for the four galaxies are shown in figure fig.canaricamimages . For display purposes, the images of NGC 2146 have been convolved with a Gaussian with of one pixel, in order to emphasise the large scale structure.

In contrast, NGC 2146 shows diffuse emission in the form of a band extending from southeast to northwest. The direction of this structure coincides with the dense dust lane identifiable in optical and near infrared images of this galaxy e.g., Martini2003 . Although this galaxy is classified as a LINER, no clear nucleus could be identified, and therefore it was left out of the AGN sample.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2146 as the nearby SB(s)ab host of SN 2018zd, adopts a distance from host-galaxy measurements, and estimates significant host extinction from Na i D absorption.

quotes.quote: Koichi Itagaki reported the discovery of the transient AT 2018zd in the nearby SB(s)ab galaxy NGC 2146 on March 02.49, 2018 (UT dates are used throughout this paper).

The distance D of SN 2018zd adopted in the following calculation is 18.4 4.5 Mpc, derived from the averaged measurements of the host galaxy (NGC 2146) listed in the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED http://ned.ipac.caltech.edu ). For example, the average estimation from the Tully-Fisher relation is D = 19.2 8.8 Mpc Bottinelli84,Giraud85,Tully88,Schoniger94,Tutui97 , the measurement via the observed angular radius of typical globular clusters in NGC 2146 (comparing with the physical radii of Milky Way globulars) is 18.0 1.8 Mpc Adamo12 , and the result from the radial velocity of NGC 2146 ( v = 1219 16 km s ^ 1 , after correcting for Local Group infall into the Virgo Cluster, the Great Attractor, and the Shapley Supercluster Mould00 ) is 17.98 1.26 Mpc with H _0 = 67.8 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 Planck14 .

Two Na i D absorption systems are detected in the spectra of SN 2018zd, with similar equivalent width (EW), 1.3 0.1 \ (see Fig. <Sp_comp> ), suggesting significant extinction due to the host galaxy and the Milky Way. The minimum reddening value derived from the existing empirical correlations between reddening and EW of Na i D, E(B-V) = 0.16 EW 0.01 mag Tura03 , is E(B-V)_ host = E(B-V)_ MW = 0.198 mag.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2146 as an edge-on starburst spiral with tidal streams, merger history, superwind, dense H II regions, dusty bulge, and subsolar SN-site metallicity.

quotes.quote: NGC 2146 is an edge-on spiral galaxy with several tidal streams that were probably ejected during a galaxy merger event 800 Myr ago (Extended Data Fig. EDfig:discovery ). The presence of a starburst-driven superwind from the bulge is revealed across the electromagnetic spectrum from rays to infrared , indicating an ongoing high star-formation rate (SFR 10 M_ yr ^ 1 ). On the basis of radio observations of the bulge , there are many more dense H ii regions (each containing up to 1000 type O6 stars) than supernova remnants, suggesting a relatively young phase of the starburst. The bulge has a high dust content and roughly solar metallicity ( 12+ log _ 10 [ O/H ] = 8.68 0.10 ) .

Since SN 2018zd is at a relatively large separation from the nucleus of 1 83 northwest ( 36 1 north, 103 7 west; Extended Data Fig. EDfig:discovery ), and the galactic radius parameter R_ 25 =1 78 (via the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database), if we reasonably assume that there is an abundance gradient for the galaxy, the metallicity at the SN site is probably subsolar; this merits future investigations once the SN fades.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 2146 as a starburst with outflow-dominated 89 um B-field orientations, roughly 4 kpc maximum outflow height, and possible tidal-tail dust from recent interaction.

quotes.quote: We present a comprehensive study of the B-fields in three nearby ( 3.5 - 17.2 Mpc) starbursts (M82, NGC 253, and NGC 2146) at 5 pc-- 1.5 kpc resolutions using publicly available 53 - 890 imaging polarimetric observations with SOFIA/HAWC+, JCMT/POL-2, and ALMA. We find that the polarized spectral energy distributions (SEDs) of the full galaxies are dominated by the polarized SEDs of the outflows with dust temperatures of T_ d,outflow ^ PI 45 K and emissive index of _ outflow ^ PI 2.3 .
At 89 , the distribution of B-field orientations is dominated by the galactic outflow in M 82 and NGC 2146, and by the disk in NGC 253.

The h_ max 5 kpc at 214 in NGC 2146 corresponds to the polarization measurement in the west side of the galaxy, which is not spatially colocated with the outflow (Figures fig:fig1 and fig:fig2 ). This polarization measurement may be part of the dust in the tidal tail due to the recent interaction that triggered star-formation in NGC 2146 Martini2003, Tarchi2004 .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2146 as a starburst merger with tidal streams, a central starburst, an extended H I reservoir, and a Ruby Ring cluster complex in one stream.

quotes.quote: In this proceeding we will present one-object study focused on the starburst galaxy NGC 2146 (Adamo et al. in prep) and the analysis of a unique ring-like cluster complex localised at the edge of one of the tidal streams of the galaxy 2012arXiv1205.6204A . The large-scale color composite image of NGC 2146 (see Figure fig2 ), made of WIYN optical broadband imaging data, directly reveals typical signatures of a merger, e.g tidal streams, intense central starburst. The "merger" nature of the galaxy is also supported by the extended H i reservoir of gas, suggesting an interaction with a low surface brightness galaxy companion 2001A&A...365..360T . Deep U-band (F336W) imaging of NGC2146 in the SHUCS program has enhanced the accessible archival HST BVIH_ data.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2146 as the lone OH65-emission case, a non-active starburst LIRG whose emission is attributed to radiative pumping rather than collisions.

quotes.quote: The galaxy with EW(OH65) emission is NGC 2146, which is a non-active galaxy, but is a bright LIRG.

The one galaxy in the sample with OH65 in emission is NGC 2146, which is a starburst galaxy, supports this idea. Based on the temperatures from the literature, the emission in NGC 2146 is likely not a result of collisional excitation. Radiative pumping is therefore the more likely method.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 2146 favors an AME SED component but classifies the signal as marginal, with a high 70-80 GHz fitted AME peak shared with NGC 2903.

quotes.quote: Combining with the ancillary data, we investigated the radio-IR spectral energy distribution (SED), while searching for the AME signals in five galaxies. The AME in NGC 2903 was well detected at a significant confidence level, whereas that in NGC 2146 and M82 was marginal. NGC 1068 and Arp 299 indicated no significant hints, and we provided the upper limits for the AME.

The statistical tests suggested that among the sample of five, NGC 2146, NGC 2903, and M82 favored the model with the AME component, which is supported by the AIC and the LR statistical tests with p-values < 0.05. Although the ^2_ n values deny the favor of the model with AME, as evident, the AME-included model described the observed SED better than the model without the AME component. This was particularly observed in the essential frequencies for the AME component, which is supported by smaller ^2 values in the AME-included model cases. In addition, results based on the convolved data are provided; however, the data of the original resolutions yielded a consistent result, favoring the model with AME in the same three targets. Although there are three galaxies for which the presence of AME was suggestive based on statistical tests, the AME in NGC 2146 and M82 were classified as marginal detections owing to the AME component being embedded in total SED and having a small signal-to-noise ratio, S/N_ AME 2.0 .

AME peak frequency vs. dust temperature From the SED fitting, the AME bumps of NGC 2146 and NGC 2903 were found to peak at 70-80 GHz, which is the first case identifying AME at such high frequencies to date.

sources.summary: NGC 2146 is described as a prototypical merger with tidal H i structures, a warped disk, an extended tidal tail, vigorous star formation, compact radio sources, and absorption features.

quotes.quote: Residual calibration errors are responsible for the ``spoke-like'' artifacts radiating from the nuclear region. This galaxy is a prototypical merger, evidenced by tidal H i structures and a warped optical disk. The streamer seen in the neutral hydrogen towards the South continues into a very extended tidal tail (Taramopoulos et al. ). The merger has resulted in vigorous star formation throughout this galaxy, resulting in a brightness comparable to that of M 82. Similar to this nearby prototype, the bright radio continuum contains numerous compact sources reflecting ultra-compact H II regions and/or radio supernovae (Tarchi et al. ).

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 2146 is detected in both H2CO transitions for the first time and links those detections with strong CO/HCN emission and a disk-like molecular structure.

quotes.quote: NGC 253, NGC 660, Maffei 2, IC 342 (all in Figure fig:NGC253NGC660Maffei2IC342FormSpec ), NGC 2146, M 82, M 82SW, NGC 3079 (all in Figure fig:NGC2146M82M82SWNGC3079FormSpec ), NGC 3628, IC 860, M 83, IR 15107+0724 (all in Figure fig:NGC3628IC860M83IR15107FormSpec ), Arp 220, and NGC 6946 (both in Figure fig:Arp220NGC6946FormSpec ) were all detected in both H _2 CO transitions.

NGC 2146: The relatively intense CO and HCN J= 1-0 emission (800 and 30 mK, respectively) measured by Gao2004a is consistent with our detections of H _2 CO 1_ 10 1_ 11 and 2_ 11 2_ 12 absorption (Figure fig:NGC2146M82M82SWNGC3079FormSpec ), the first reported detection of this molecule in this galaxy. Greve2006 imaged the ^ 12 CO J= 1-0 and J= 2-1 and ^ 13 CO J= 1-0 emission from this barred spiral galaxy, measuring a disk-like structure 5^ 20^ in extent.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2146 as a dusty starburst LIRG with strong dust lanes, strong stellar winds, and good agreement between stellar-continuum and Balmer-decrement extinction maps.

quotes.quote: NGC 2146 and NGC 7331 have particularly strong dust lane features.
NGC 2146 is classified as a luminous infrared galaxy (LIRG) Sanders2003 and has been identified as having strong stellar winds as part of a starburst, similar to M82.

NGC 2146 shows fairly good agreement between the two A _V measures, suggesting either better mixing of the dust with the stars or a completely foreground preponderance of dust, both of which may be caused by the strong starburst, winds and shocks observed in this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2146 as one of the KINGFISH starbursts, finds a high central ISRF, and flags its dusty outflow as a model outlier.

quotes.quote: Only NGC 2146 and NGC 1097 might be considered ``starbursts'' in terms of their specific SFR (SFR divided by stellar mass, sSFR) of 0.5 Gyr ^ 1 ; most of the remaining galaxies are star-forming galaxies along the ``main sequence'' of star formation salim07,noeske07,karim11 .

The radial profiles probe a wide range of ISRF levels; \ can be as high as 20 in the centers of NGC 1377 This galaxy is optically thick for 30 \ so the DL07 models may not be strictly appropriate because of the inherent assumption that the dust emission is optically thin. and NGC 2146, but falls to = 0.1 in the centers of dwarf galaxies (e.g., DDO 154, DDO 165, Holmberg II, IC 2574, M 81 Dwarf B) and in the outskirts of virtually all spirals and early-type galaxies.

The single outlier, the central point of NGC 2146, does not follow this trend, but this galaxy contains a dusty outflow along the minor axis heckman00,kreckel14 so these (optically thin) models may not be applicable.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2146 as a nearby starburst whose intense activity is linked to interaction or merger history, and whose spectrum shows pronounced steepening and thermal flattening.

quotes.quote: NGC 2146 and NGC 3034 (M 82) are classical nearby starburst galaxies, again with the intense star-forming activity resulting from gravitational interaction with a nearby galaxy or from a past merger event. A comprehensive radio continuum study of NGC 2146 was reported by 1996MNRAS.281..301L , who investigated the cosmic-ray transport in this galaxy.

In addition, the broad radio range covered by our data in most of these galaxies renders both, the steepening of the spectrum starting at 1 - 5 GHz, due to the strong curvature of the synchrotron emission, and the subsequent flattening due to the dominance of the thermal radio emission (above 10 GHz) visible in the measured spectra The most noticeable cases are NGC 2146, NGC 4449, NGC 4631, and NGC 5194, but also in NGC 1569, NGC 3034, and NGC 3310 this trend is visible.

sources.summary: The catalog paper reports NGC 2146 as a post-3FGL LAT detection and favors its 4FGL association despite a nearby FSRQ because variability is absent and the photon index is low.

quotes.quote: Two sources, Arp 220 Pen16,Gri16,Yoa17 and NGC 2146 Tan14 , have been reported as LAT detections since the 3FGL release.

NGC 2146 being close ( 0 1 ) to the FSRQ 1REX J061757+7816.1, the association is ambiguous. We favor the NGC 2146 association as no evidence for variability is found and the 4FGL photon index (2.17 0.17) is somewhat low for a FSRQ.

sources.summary: The review describes NGC 2146 as a peculiar spiral with northern and southern H I streams, probably from a tidal encounter with an unseen gas-rich LSB companion.

quotes.quote: NGC 2146 . --- Taramopulos et al. (2001) detect extended H i streams to the north and south of the peculiar spiral NGC 2146, possibly formed by a tidal interaction with an unseen H i rich LSB companion 800 Myr ago. They derive an H i mass of 6.2 10^9 M _ for the system, half of which resides in the southern stream. The northern stream and the galaxy contain approximately one quarter of the mass each.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2146 gamma-ray emission as star-formation dominated, says its observed spectral index favors the spectrally resolved model, and finds its total spectrum consistent with hadronic spec emission.

quotes.quote: While the gamma-ray emission from the LMC, NGC 253, M82, NGC 2146, Arp229 and Arp220 is probably dominated by emission resulting from star-formation activity, we note that some galaxies of the sample are suspected to exhibit AGN activity.
Only the observed _ of NGC 2146 slightly favours the `spec' model that exhibits a harder spectrum in comparison to the steady-state approach.
The total gamma-ray spectra of the star-forming galaxy NGC 2146 is largely consistent with the hadronic emission from our `spec' model.

sources.summary: The paper maps NGC2146 with NIKA2, treating it as a starburst spiral whose central dust mass peaks and whose thick ISM moderates dust heating.

quotes.quote: This study presents the first millimeter continuum mapping observations of two nearby galaxies, the starburst spiral galaxy NGC2146 and the dwarf galaxy NGC2976, at 1.15 mm and 2 mm using the NIKA2 camera on the IRAM 30m telescope, as part of the Guaranteed Time Large Project IMEGIN.
In NGC2146, the dust mass peaks at the central region, and the inner 1 kpc of the disk contains 40 of the total dust mass in this galaxy.
On the contrary, a thicker ISM in a starburst spiral galaxy like NGC2146 means more efficient shielding of dust grains from an energetic radiation field and a slower heating rate Remy+2015 .

gmw_00000844.name: SDSS J152156.48+520238.5
gmw_00000844.overview: SDSS J152156.48+520238.5 is an exceptionally luminous, radio-quiet quasar located at a redshift of approximately 2.2 [arXiv:1503.02085, arXiv:1502.07767]. It exhibits X-ray weakness and a flat effective photon index, which are likely caused by strong X-ray absorption [arXiv:1503.02085].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J1521+5202 as an exceptionally luminous PHL 1811 analog whose deeper Chandra spectrum shows a hard effective photon index and significant X-ray absorption.

quotes.quote: J1521+5202 is an exceptionally luminous quasar (e.g., Figure fig-lz ). However, it is remarkably X-ray weak (by a factor of 35 ), as first reported by Just2007 using a 4 ks \ observation. As a PHL 1811 analog, J1521+5202 was studied in W11, which also presented a near-infrared (NIR) spectrum showing very weak [ O iii ] emission and a higher optical Fe ii /H ratio than those of typical quasars.

With our much longer \ observation (37.1 ks cleaned exposure) of J1521+5202, we derived a band ratio of 1.1_ 0.2 ^ +0.3 and an effective power-law photon index of 0.6 0.2 (Table 1). This flat effective photon index, compared to the typical value of 1.9 for luminous radio-quiet quasars e.g., Reeves1997,Just2007,Scott2011 , suggests that the significant X-ray weakness of this source is likely due to strong absorption.
The spectral analysis also suggests significant X-ray absorption in J1521+5202, in agreement with the simple flat _ eff argument above.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J152156.48+520238.5 as an exceptionally luminous z=2.208 quasar and models its broad H-line spectroastrometric signal using a rotating disk BLR.

quotes.quote: SDSS J152156.48+520238.5 (hereafter J1521+5202) is the most luminous SDSS quasar where \ falls in the K-band (Fig. fig: angular size ). J1521+5202 has =10^ 47.6 , =111 , and z=2.208 . The of its \ line is 9300 ( Shen+11 ).

In order to estimate S_v , the broad emission line is modeled as a rotating disk with /( )=1 and either f() ^ 2 , or the f() based on . We assume for simplicity that the angular size of the emitting region is , though the true may be higher since was determined on , and \ is sometimes observed to have longer RM lags than \ ( Bentz+10 ).

sources.summary: The paper estimates scattering profiles for the high-redshift SDSS quasar, finding electron scattering detectable to 150 pkpc before dust scattering dominates at larger distances.

quotes.quote: Here we apply our formalism to two real hyper-luminous quasars, the radio-loud source 3C 273 at z=0.158 in sec:3c273 , and the quasar SDSS J152156+520238 at z=2.208 in sec:UM287 , and compare the results.

We now estimate the scattering surface brightness profiles for the hyper-luminous SDSS quasar J152156.48+520238.5 at redshift z=2.208 Schneider2005 . This object has an apparent i band magnitude m_i=15.323 mag ( M_ 1450 29.3 mag), being the brightest quasar in the SDSS catalog at this redshift Stern2015 . Figure fig:sdssqso displays the resulting profiles with our default observational settings detailed in sec:obs , where the electron scattering signal appears detectable out to 150 pkpc. At distances beyond 115 pkpc, however, the dust scattering emission profile overwhelms that from electrons and the two signals are indistinguishable beyond 90 pkpc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J152156.48+520238.5 as one of the most luminous SDSS DR7 quasars, with high black hole mass and Eddington ratio, though C IV virial values are cautioned.

quotes.quote: The highest luminosity is measured for SDSS J152156.48+520238.5 with L = 48.1 , which is one of the four most luminous quasars in the SDSS DR7 quasar catalogue. The luminosity L = 48.29 of the most luminous quasar, SDSS J074521.78+473436.1, is only marginally higher. Also the black hole mass M = 1.3 10^ 10 M_ and the Eddington ratio = 0.95 of SDSS J152156.48+520238.5 are very high, whereas the accretion rate M = 2.1 M_ / yr is rather normal. However, these values should be interpreted with caution because the virial mass is derived from the C iv line (see Sect. sec:selection ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J152156.48+520238.5 as the third most luminous catalog entry, with low equivalent-width emission lines, unresolved imaging, and no FIRST or RASS detection.

quotes.quote: Four objects have M_i < -30.0 , including SDSS J152156.48+520238.5 (see previous section), which, at M_i = -30.19 , is the third most luminous catalog entry. The spectrum of this object possesses a number of low equivalent width emission lines, which is expected from the Baldwin (1977) effect. The image of the quasar is unresolved, so if it is lensed the image separation must be considerably less than one arcsecond (see Pindor et al. 2003). This object is not seen in the FIRST or the RASS databases.

sources.summary: The paper discusses SDSS J1521+5202 as an extraordinarily luminous WLQ whose X-ray spectrum supports absorption and motivated new observations of its spectral model.

quotes.quote: In Appendix A, we briefly present the results from new \ observations of the extraordinarily luminous WLQ SDSS J1521+5202.
The presence of such X-ray absorption is further supported by a \ spectrum of an extremely luminous z = 2.238 WLQ, SDSS J1521+5202 (see Section 3.2 of Luo2015 ).
Thus, we proposed an \ observation of SDSS J1521+5202 to further study whether its X-ray spectrum could be better fit with a Compton-thick reflection model or a simple absorbed power-law model.

gmw_00000845.name: SDSS J154019.57-020505.4
gmw_00000845.overview: SDSS J154019.57-020505.4 is a quasar located at a redshift of z=0.3 [arXiv:1502.07767]. It has a bolometric luminosity of 10^45.4 erg/s and exhibits a broad H-beta emission line [arXiv:1502.07767].

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J154019.57-020505.4 as a z=0.3 quasar template whose Balmer-line width and photon flux are scaled to simulate the higher-redshift target spectrum.

quotes.quote: As the NIR spectrum of J1521+5202 is not available, we use as a template the spectrum of SDSS J154019.57-020505.4 (hereafter J1540-0205), a =10^ 45.4 quasar at z=0.3 with ( )=9730 . The two spectra should be similar since the of the Balmer lines is correlated with the of \ ( Shen+08 ). We multiply the observed photon flux of J1540-0205 by the photon flux ratio of the two quasars, 10^ 47.6 /10^ 45.4 ( (z=2.2)/ (z=0.3))^2 (1+2.2)/(1+0.3)=3.1 . The normalized spectrum is shown in the top panel of Figure fig: expected signal , together with the broad , narrow lines and continuum fits described in , which we utilize below.

gmw_00000846.name: Deep3a-15504
gmw_00000846.overview: Deep3a-15504 is a massive, high-redshift star-forming disk galaxy containing a central bulge and several star-forming clumps [arXiv:0802.0879, arXiv:1802.07276]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus that drives a nuclear outflow, while its disk exhibits signatures of radial gas inflow and a minor companion at its northern edge [arXiv:2109.02659, arXiv:1802.07276].

sources.summary: The paper highlights D3a-15504 as a 2D kinematic case with radial inflow signatures, central residual bimodality, large-scale twist, and a central dispersion excess from nuclear outflow.

quotes.quote: To illustrate the secondary signatures from noncircular motions seen in the 2D kinematics, we examine the case of D3a-15504, for which the kinematics show some of the most suggestive features of radial inflow Genzel06 . We construct a toy model combining disk rotation with radial inflow as follows. We first subtract the best-fit galaxy+halo model (determined from the 1D MCMC fits) from the 2D AO kinematic maps, yielding initial velocity and dispersion residual maps. For this galaxy, the velocity residual (Figure fig:example_radial_2D , top left) shows a central residual bimodality, and a larger-scale bimodal ``twist''. The dispersion residual shows a large central excess, which primarily reflects the nuclear outflow seen in this galaxy ( Genzel06 , ForsterSchreiber14 ).

We find that a constant radial inflow component of v_ 90 km \ s^ 1 can explain much of the original kinematic residuals (see Figure fig:example_radial_2D , last column), removing much of the large-scale ``S'' twist from NE to SW and the central velocity bimodality seen in the galaxy+halo-only velocity residual map. The remaining minor-axis velocity bimodality and central dispersion peak are likely due to the AGN-driven outflow in this galaxy (as noted in Genzel06 ), but we do not attempt to model the outflow for this toy model.

sources.summary: The paper describes BzK-15504 as a massive z~2 star-forming disk with rapid continuous star formation, no recent major merger, and a minor-axis inflow signature.

quotes.quote: In the case of a typical massive ( 10^ 11 ) star-forming galaxy, BzK-15504, which Gen+06 studied in detail, modeling of the spectral energy distribution (SED) from broad band photometry was used to measure the stellar mass and age of the system. With the stellar mass M _* 8 10^ 10 \ and the star formation rate of 100 \ (as measured from ), together with the assumption of a constant star formation rate, all the stars in this system could have been formed in 500 Myr Gen+06 . This number agrees well with the stellar age measured from the SED fitting ( 300-800 Myr), making it likely that this system formed rapidly with continuous star formation (and therefore mass inflow) at its current rate Gen+06 .

We also observe such an inflow in a SINS system, BzK-15504, as described by Gen+06 . In this system, a strong velocity gradient is also seen along the minor axis, in the form of a high A_ 1,v term, presumably corresponding to the driving of fuel towards a growing bulge with an embedded active nucleus Gen+06 . We can nevertheless robustly identify both the ``redshifted" NGC 4579 and BzK-15504 as disks, since the kinemetric signature of the inflows is restricted to the A_ 1,v coefficient, which is excluded from our analysis for precisely this reason (and could potentially be used in future work as a tracer of AGN feedback at high redshift).

sources.summary: The paper describes Deep3a-15504 as a deeply observed massive rotating disk with a bulge, star-forming clumps, AGN-driven nuclear outflow, outer rotation-curve decline, and a minor companion.

quotes.quote: Deep3a-15504 --- This galaxy was the first object we observed with SINFONI + AO and has the deepest data of our survey (23 h). It is a large high-mass rotating disk with a massive bulge seen in rest-optical continuum light and stellar mass map causing a steep inner velocity gradient, several modestly bright H emitting star-forming clumps are distributed across the disk, and an AGN revealed by diagnostic rest-UV and optical line emission drives a nuclear outflow that is spatially extended and anisotropic in broad H + [ N 2 ] emission Gen06, Gen08, Gen14a, FS14, Tacc15a .
